content.assessment: T4 deliberately avoids structural change, which the Polish case suggests is a fundamentally different reform category from Poland's tracking-age interventions. The lens criteria of structural mechanism and distributional effects are most informative here by contrast: Poland's evidence indicates that removing or delaying the tracking mechanism itself, not just compensatory funding within an unchanged structure, produced the documented socio-economic gap reductions.
opportunities: Because it requires no legal restructuring, T4 sidesteps the implementation-sequencing risks Poland encountered entirely, offering a stable, low-risk complement to (rather than substitute for) structural reform
strengths: By leaving structure unchanged, T4 avoids any of the transition/reversal risks documented in the Polish 2017 case (F-live-polish_reform-04, F-live-polish_reform-05)
threats: If Polish-style evidence is generalizable at all, the absence of structural change limits T4's potential to replicate Poland's gap-reduction outcomes, since compensatory measures were not the operative mechanism in the Polish case (F-live-polish_reform-03)
weaknesses: Poland's 1999 reform's documented reduction of socio-economic performance gaps was attributed specifically to structural delay of tracking, not to compensatory funding alone, suggesting T4's non-structural approach may be a categorically weaker lever for the same distributional goal (F-live-polish_reform-03)
